# Harry and Meghan return for a 'quasi-royal' tour of Australia, with a commercial bent

- [![A man with grey hair and beard in a blue suit and tie stands in a TV studio.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/51eb846e120c8743d9a19dcc52a48f79?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2667&cropW=2667&xPos=1079&yPos=203&width=160&height=160)\\
\\
B\\
\\
By Ben Knight](https://www.abc.net.au/news/ben-knight/166868)
- [Topic:Royalty](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/royalty)

4h ago4 hours agoMon 13 Apr 2026 at 3:23pm

![prince harry and his wife Meghan leaving a cathedral in the UK smiling and happy.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/3d20521b2b27b55d6f4afcb85af5eb22?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2020&cropW=2693&xPos=153&yPos=0&width=862&height=647)

The couple retains the right to use the Duke and Duchess of Sussex titles despite distancing themselves from the British royal family. (AP: Frank Augstein, file photo)

The last time the Sussexes visited Australia in 2018, they were newly married, popular and still well within the royal fold.

None of those things is true any more.

Even so, this still looks very much like a royal visit — although officially, it very much is not.

Harry and Meghan will be travelling under their titles of Duke and Duchess of Sussex, and spending much of the week making the rounds of various charities and causes they have aligned themselves with.

![Britain's Prince Harry and Meghan, Duchess of Sussex wave to the well-wishers after taking a ride on a tram in Melbourne.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/72892a60ae50fa875a48dc22776c7b23?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=917&cropW=1372&xPos=68&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

The well-received 2018 trip took place just after the couple announced they were expecting their first child. (AAP: William West)

It begins today at Melbourne's Royal Children's Hospital, followed by a visit to a women's homeless and family violence shelter by the duchess, then an event this evening with the families of war veterans.

Tomorrow, Harry will appear at a Movember event at Melbourne's Whitten Oval with players from the Western Bulldogs AFL team, before flying to Canberra for events at the Australian War Memorial — where he will meet Indigenous veterans and attend a Last Post ceremony.

![Fans stretch their hands out to try and shake hands with Prince Harry.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/fb0c92ebb70399ef139590a9af3852de?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1909&cropW=2871&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

A lot has changed since Prince Harry greeted crowds in Australia in 2018. (ABC News: Danielle Bonica)

On Thursday, they will be in Melbourne again, attending Indigenous and mental health events, before heading to Sydney on Friday to promote Invictus Australia, the veterans' charity he founded.

But there are some significant differences to a standard royal tour — most notably, the commercial aspect.

According to a media statement issued before their arrival in Australia, the pair's program "will focus on mental health, community resilience, and support for veterans and their families, alongside private meetings and special projects".

Those "private meetings and special projects" include two major, ticketed events.

Harry will deliver a speech at a conference in Melbourne on Thursday, while Meghan is the star attraction at a luxury wellness event at Coogee Beach in Sydney next weekend.

A ticket to Meghan's event, branded "a girls' weekend like no other" and involving a gala dinner, yoga, meditation and "sound healing", will set participants back between $2,699 and $3,199.

![Prince Harry and his wife Meghan walk at Kingfisher Bay Resort on Fraser Island.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/651f47653538ccce84cfa6bb74954ae4?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1331&cropW=2000&xPos=0&yPos=40&width=862&height=575)

The couple's day-to-day schedule will differ considerably from their official royal tour seven years ago. (AAP: Darren England)

According to the media statement, the Sussexes have included private events "to support broader commercial, charitable, and community objectives".

"These are separate from the core programme of public-facing visits."

So, is it a publicity tour?

The media statement also poses, and responds, to that question like this:

"The programme is rooted in long-standing areas of work for the Duke and Duchess, with a clear focus on amplifying organisations delivering measurable impact. The visit prioritises listening, learning and supporting communities rather than promotion."

## Excitement likely to be dulled around 'quasi-royal tour'

Royal expert Giselle Bastin, from Flinders University, described the commercial aspect of the tour as "unusual" and said the royals would not be impressed that the Sussexes were "monetising their visit to Australia".

"The fact that they are not working royals and are staging this visit as a quasi-royal tour would leave the Windsors nonplussed," she said.

> "I suspect there will be many Australians — who were once big fans of Harry and Meghan — who will now be more sceptical of their motives."

![Harry, Meghan and Archie insideWindsor Castle and surrounded by family for an official portrait](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/2efe3eee392dfc6991d7532bc41a3db8?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1920&cropW=2889&xPos=25&yPos=702&width=862&height=575)

The royals together mid-2019 for the christening of Harry and Meghan's son, Archie. (@SussexRoyal: Chris Allerton, AP)

She said the level of excitement surrounding their 2018 visit was likely to be much more restrained during this one.

"Both Prince Harry and Meghan were greeted by many Australians with considerable excitement in 2018, but I suspect — while there will be many royal fans still thrilled to see the couple — there won't be as much excitement this time", she said.

"Since 2018, Harry and Meghan have ceased to be working royals, moved to California, and spent countless hours in media outlets expressing their dislike for the royal system, for particular royals, and for Britain and its tabloid culture of racism and sexism.

"They are thought to have cast a shadow over the final years of the late Queen Elizabeth II and the Duke of Edinburgh, and for this they have attracted the ire of many a royal enthusiast."

![Woman with pink sign reading "Harry I wagged school in 1983 to meet your parents"](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/b93d08eb39d634cdfe93cff88cad7c83?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1920&cropW=2877&xPos=1&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Royal enthusiasts may feel different about the visit this time around. (ABC News: Danielle Bonica)

British journalist and royal commentator Afua Hagan said the couple faced criticism whatever they did and would probably be vilified if they did not monetise their visit.

"If they didn't have commercial interests then the problem would be, oh my goodness, these people are leeching off the royal family and the taxpayers," she said.

Ms Hagan said Harry and Meghan were favourite targets of the press in Britain and how they handled their Australian trip would be unlikely to change that.

"The negative press when it comes to Harry and Meghan is what sells the tabloids, it's what sells the papers," she said.

"You know, you have a have a villain in the story."

In the UK, [favourability trackers](https://yougov.com/en-gb/articles/53895-royal-family-favourability-trackers-january-2026) suggest just 31 per cent and 19 per cent of the public have a positive view of Harry and Meghan, respectively, with the couple's ratings only higher than disgraced Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor.

Dr Bastin said the pair's tour down under was being poorly perceived in the UK.

"The tabloid press is having its usual field day with the way the Sussexes have not 'read the room' with this visit, especially as it comes just before King Charles III is due to visit the USA for the 250th celebration of American Independence," she said.

The prince has long criticised the UK's tabloid press, [telling a British court this year](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-01-22/prince-harry-gives-evidence-at-high-court-in-privacy-case/106255216) certain outlets had left him "paranoid beyond belief" while making his wife's life "an absolute misery".
